issues.original: Most tutorials show you a 5-line Dockerfile and move on. Then, when you deploy to production, you find your image is 1.2 GB, builds take 8 minutes even for a one-line code change, and your security team flags vulnerabilities in packages you didn't even know were installed. Writing a good Dockerfile is a skill that pays off every time your CI pipeline runs.
issues.fixed: Most tutorials show you a 5-line Dockerfile and move on. When you deploy to production, you might find your image is 1.2 GB, builds take 8 minutes even for a one-line code change, and your security team flags vulnerabilities in packages you didn't know were installed. Writing a good Dockerfile is a skill that pays off every time your CI pipeline runs.

issues.original: Every Dockerfile begins with [CODE]. It sets the base image that all subsequent instructions build upon.
issues.fixed: Every Dockerfile starts with [CODE], which sets the base image for all subsequent instructions.

issues.original: Always pin a specific version. [CODE] will break when a new Python version is released and your code isn't compatible.
issues.fixed: Always pin a specific version. [CODE] will break if a new Python version is released and your code isn't compatible.

issues.original: Each [CODE] creates a new layer.
issues.fixed: Each [CODE] creates a new layer.

issues.original: If you [CODE] in one [CODE] and [CODE] in another, the package lists still exist in the first layer — layers are additive, never subtractive.
issues.fixed: If you [CODE] in one [CODE] and [CODE] in another, the package lists still exist in the first layer because layers are additive, not subtractive.

issues.original: Use [CODE] unless you specifically need tar extraction. [CODE] is more explicit and predictable.
issues.fixed: Use [CODE] unless you specifically need tar extraction. [CODE] is more explicit and predictable.

issues.original: Neither [CODE] nor [CODE] should contain secrets. Build arguments are stored in the image history ([CODE]). Use Docker secrets or mount secrets at build time with [CODE] instead.
issues.fixed: Neither [CODE] nor [CODE] should contain secrets. Build arguments are stored in the image history ([CODE]). Use Docker secrets or mount secrets at build time with [CODE] instead.

issues.original: It's documentation. You still need [CODE] when running the container. Think of it as a comment that tooling can read.
issues.fixed: It's documentation. You still need [CODE] when running the container. Think of it as a comment that tooling can read.

issues.original: We'll cover why this matters for security in article 7.
issues.fixed: We'll cover why this matters for security in article 7.

issues.original: Docker marks the container as [CODE], [CODE], or [CODE] based on the healthcheck results. Orchestrators (Docker Compose, Kubernetes) use this to decide whether to route traffic to the container.
issues.fixed: Docker marks the container as [CODE], [CODE], or [CODE] based on the healthcheck results. Orchestrators like Docker Compose and Kubernetes use this to decide whether to route traffic to the container.

issues.original: The [CODE] file works like [CODE] but for the Docker build context. When you run [CODE], Docker sends the entire directory (the "build context") to the daemon. Without [CODE], this includes everything.
issues.fixed: The [CODE] file works like [CODE] but for the Docker build context. When you run [CODE], Docker sends the entire directory (the "build context") to the daemon. Without [CODE], this includes everything.

issues.original: You can verify cache behavior in the build output: 42 seconds saved because [CODE] didn't change.
issues.fixed: You can verify cache behavior in the build output: 42 seconds saved because [CODE] didn't change.

issues.original: Containers are deliberately isolated. That's the point. But useful applications need to accept connections from the outside world, talk to databases, and store data that survives container restarts. Docker provides two mechanisms for this: networks (for communication) and volumes (for persistent storage). Getting these right is the difference between a demo and a deployment.
issues.fixed: Containers are deliberately isolated. That's the point. But useful applications need to accept connections from the outside world, talk to databases, and store data that survives container restarts. Docker provides two mechanisms for this: networks (for communication) and volumes (for persistent storage). Getting these right makes the difference between a demo and a deployment.

issues.original: When Docker starts, it creates a virtual network infrastructure on the host. Each container gets its own network namespace (with its own IP address, routing table, and network interfaces), and Docker manages the traffic flow between containers and the outside world.
issues.fixed: When Docker starts, it creates a virtual network infrastructure on the host. Each container gets its own network namespace (with its own IP address, routing table, and network interfaces), and Docker manages the traffic flow between containers and the outside world.

issues.original: For most single-host use cases, you'll use **bridge** networks. Let's explore the relevant drivers.
issues.fixed: For most single-host use cases, you'll use **bridge** networks. Let's explore the relevant drivers.

issues.original: Every container that doesn't specify a network joins the default bridge. Let's see it in action:
issues.fixed: Every container that doesn't specify a network joins the default bridge. Let's see it in action:

issues.original: This is a deliberate limitation of the default bridge. For service discovery, you need a custom bridge network.
issues.fixed: This is a deliberate limitation of the default bridge. For service discovery, you need a custom bridge network.

issues.original: Custom bridge networks provide automatic DNS resolution between containers — this is what you should use for applications.
issues.fixed: Custom bridge networks provide automatic DNS resolution between containers — this is what you should use for applications.

issues.original: The container name [CODE] resolves to its IP address. This is Docker's built-in DNS server at work. Docker runs an embedded DNS server at [CODE] inside each container on a custom network.
issues.fixed: The container name [CODE] resolves to its IP address. This is Docker's built-in DNS server at work. Docker runs an embedded DNS server at [CODE] inside each container on a custom network.

issues.original: Custom networks also provide isolation — containers on different networks can't communicate unless explicitly connected.
issues.fixed: Custom networks also provide isolation — containers on different networks can't communicate unless explicitly connected.

issues.original: **Bottom line**: Always create custom bridge networks for your applications. The default bridge is for backward compatibility.
issues.fixed: **Bottom line**: Always create custom bridge networks for your applications. The default bridge is for backward compatibility.

issues.original: Containers have their own network namespace. To expose a container's port to the host (and thus the outside world), you map ports.
issues.fixed: Containers have their own network namespace. To expose a container's port to the host (and thus the outside world), you map ports.

issues.original: The format is always [CODE]. Read it as 'host port 8080 forwards to container port 80.'
issues.fixed: The format is always [CODE]. Read it as 'host port 8080 forwards to container port 80.'

issues.original: The [CODE] network mode removes network isolation entirely — the container uses the host's network stack directly:
issues.fixed: The [CODE] network mode removes network isolation entirely — the container uses the host's network stack directly:

issues.original: This eliminates the overhead of port mapping and NAT, providing slightly better network performance. The tradeoff: you lose port isolation (two containers can't both listen on port 80), and the container can see all host network interfaces.
issues.fixed: This eliminates the overhead of port mapping and NAT, providing slightly better network performance. The tradeoff: you lose port isolation (two containers can't both listen on port 80), and the container can see all host network interfaces.

issues.original: Use host networking when: - You need maximum network performance - Your application binds to many ports dynamically - You're running monitoring tools that need to see host network traffic
issues.fixed: Use host networking when: - You need maximum network performance - Your application binds to many ports dynamically - You're running monitoring tools that need to see host network traffic

issues.original: By default, all data written inside a container is stored in its writable layer. When the container is deleted, that data is gone. Volumes provide persistent storage that exists independently of the container lifecycle.
issues.fixed: By default, all data written inside a container is stored in its writable layer. When the container is deleted, that data is gone. Volumes provide persistent storage that exists independently of the container lifecycle.

issues.original: Docker supports three ways to mount storage into a container.
issues.fixed: Docker supports three ways to mount storage into a container.

issues.original: Named volumes are the recommended way to persist data. Docker manages the storage location, and volumes can be shared between containers.
issues.fixed: Named volumes are the recommended way to persist data. Docker manages the storage location, and volumes can be shared between containers.

issues.original: Bind mounts map a specific host directory into the container. They're essential for development workflows.
issues.fixed: Bind mounts map a specific host directory into the container. They're essential for development workflows.

issues.original: Any changes to files on the host are immediately visible in the container (and vice versa). This enables live development without rebuilding the image.
issues.fixed: Any changes to files on the host are immediately visible in the container (and vice versa). This enables live development without rebuilding the image.

issues.original: A container that works is invisible. A container that doesn't work is a black box. The entire point of containerization is isolation — but that same isolation makes debugging harder. You can't just [CODE] into a container or browse its filesystem from the host. Docker provides a specific set of tools for inspecting, diagnosing, and understanding what happens inside running (and crashed) containers.
issues.fixed: A working container is invisible. A non-working one is a black box. The main goal of containerization is isolation, but this isolation also makes debugging more challenging. You can't just [CODE] into a container or browse its filesystem from the host. Docker offers specific tools for inspecting, diagnosing, and understanding what happens inside running and crashed containers.

issues.original: The container exited with code 1 (error). The logs show it couldn't connect to PostgreSQL. This could be because the database wasn't ready when the app started (a dependency ordering issue) or the hostname was incorrect.
issues.fixed: The container exited with code 1 (error). The logs show it couldn't connect to PostgreSQL. This could be because the database wasn't ready when the app started (a dependency ordering issue) or the hostname was wrong.

issues.original: Minimal images (Alpine, distroless) might not have bash or common tools. For distroless images, you can't exec into them at all (no shell). Use a debug sidecar instead.
issues.fixed: Minimal images (Alpine, distroless) might lack bash or common tools. For distroless images, you can't exec into them (no shell). Use a debug sidecar instead.

issues.original: Without log rotation, [CODE] logs grow unbounded and will eventually fill your disk:
issues.fixed: Without log rotation, [CODE] logs grow unbounded and will eventually fill your disk.
